output:
**Yes, Campaign B maintains effective recall significantly longer.**

Solving R(t) = 20% for each campaign:

**Campaign A** (R₀=80%, τ=30):
```
0.20 = 0.80 · e^(-t/30)
e^(-t/30) = 0.25
t = -30 · ln(0.25) = 30 · ln(4) ≈ 41.6 days
```

**Campaign B** (R₀=40%, τ=90):
```
0.20 = 0.40 · e^(-t/90)
e^(-t/90) = 0.50
t = -90 · ln(0.50) = 90 · ln(2) ≈ 62.4 days
```

**Campaign B stays above 20% for ~62 days vs. Campaign A's ~42 days — roughly 21 days longer.**

The intuition: τ (the decay constant) matters more than R₀ for long-term effectiveness. Campaign A starts at 80% but decays fast (τ=30), losing half its recall every ~21 days. Campaign B starts at just 40% but decays three times slower (τ=90), losing half every ~62 days. The slower decay more than compensates for the lower starting point.

This is a common pattern in marketing — high-reach campaigns create a spike that fades quickly, while sustained/targeted campaigns build slower but more durable brand memory.
